coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon quantum dots decorated self-healing hydrogel for highly effective treatment of superbug infected wounds
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Shengli
Chen, Zhizhao
Tu, Hanyu
Liu, Huaxin
Liu, Yang
Chen, Shuyue
Cai, Dongyang
Liu, Can
Zhang, Xiangyan
Zou, Guoqiang
Xie, Huiqing
Ji, Xiaobo
Hou, Hongshuai
Zhou, Jianda
coredata.dc:description: Rapid healing of wounds infected by bacteria, especially multidrug-resistant bacteria, is a common clinical challenge. Local bacterial infection causes excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) that further induce oxidative stress and inflammatory responses, which impairs cell migration, angiogenesis and delays cutaneous regeneration. Herein, we designed a novel type of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from herbal medicines. The CQDs are able to against gram-positive bacteria including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and possessed SOD-like as well as CAT-like activities for scavenging ROS. The biocompatible CQDs could protect HUVECs migration and angiogenesis from oxidative stress damage. Moreover, the expression of NO and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) of LPS-induced macrophages could be downregulated by the CQDs. Besides, the self-healing hydrogel decorated with multifunctional CQDs were prepared via the reaction between OSA and CMCS through Schiff base linkage for better wound management. The CQDs/hydrogel could continuously eliminate bacteria and scavenge ROS to modulate the complex oxidative stress microenvironment. The results of animal experiments demonstrated that the CQDs/hydrogel exerted the effects of eliminating MRSA, anti-inflammation, and promoting angiogenesis, which was manifested in enhancing wound healing, epithelial regeneration and collagen disposition. Therefore, the CQDs/hydrogel is excellent candidate for MRSA-infected skin wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Antibacterial
Self-healing hydrogel
Nanozymes
MRSA-infected wound healing

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intracellular anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory activity test
Migration and angiogenesis tests of HUVECs
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and hydrogel
In vitro anti-bacterial and anti-oxidative capacity
Cytotoxicity, intracellular anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects
In vitro cell migration and angiogenesis
In vivo CQDs/hydrogel promotes MRSA-infected wound healing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of CQDs. a, b) TEM images of CQDs, c) Particle size distribution of CQDs with superimposed Gaussian fits (black traces), d) UV–vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of CQDs. Photographs of CQDs taken under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right) (inset), e) FTIR spectra of CQDs, f) Full scan XPS spectra of CQDs, high resolution of g) C1s, h) N1s and i) O1s spectra of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Preparation and characterization of hydrogel. a) Photographs of the CQDs/OSA solution, CMCS solution and CQDs/OSA/CMCS hydrogel at room temperature. Scale bar: 0.5 cm. b) Self-healing performance of hydrogels. Scale bar: 0.5 cm. c) SEM images of freeze-dried pure hydrogel (c1) and CQDs/hydrogel (c2). d) Confocal luminescence images of CQDs/hydrogel. e) Time dependence of storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) of hydrogel. f) Viscosity values with shear rate variety of hydrogel. g) The change of G′ and G″ with cyclic strain from 1% to 700% with 1 min for every strain interval.

In Vitro Anti-bacterial and Anti-oxidative Capacity. Images of agar plates with a1) S. aureus and b1) MRSA, Scale bar: 1 cm. Fluorescence images of live/dead bacterial staining of a2) S. aureus and b2) MRSA, Scale bar: 20 µm. Statistical analysis of relative bacterial viability of c) S. aureus and d) MRSA according to the plate colony counting. e) ABTS inhibition ratio of CQDs. c) •O2 − inhibition ratio of CQDs. d) H2O2 inhibition ratio of CQDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001.

In vitro biocompatibility of CQDs. a) Cytotoxicity of CQDs (μg/mL) on HUVECs. b) Cytotoxicity of CQDs (μg/mL) on RAW264.7. c) Percentage of Ki67+ cells in Fig. 4e. d) Calcein-AM and PI staining in HUVECs. Green signals for live cells, while red for the dead. Scale bar: 100 μm. e) Immunofluorescence of Ki67 in HUVECs. Scale bar: 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cells protection from ROS attack via CQDs. a) Detection of CQDs reducing the ROS level in a H2O2 -stimulated HUVECs model by fluorescence microscopy. Scale bar: 100 μm. b) Detection of CQDs reducing the NO level in a LPS -stimulated RAW264.7 model by fluorescence microscopy. Scale bar: 100 μm. c) Quantitative analysis of HUVECs after DCFH-DA staining in Fig. 5a. d) Quantitative analysis of HUVECs after DAF-2-DA staining in Fig. 5b. e) ELISA assessment of TNF-α levels. f) ELISA assessment of IL-6 levels. g) Scratch assay images of HUVECs. Scale bar: 100 μm. h) Matrigel angiogenesis assay images of HUVECs. Scale bar: 100 μm. i) Cell migration quantification of HUVECs. j, k) Tube formation quantification of HUVECs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 4, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001.

In Vivo CQDs/Hydrogel Promotes MRSA-infected Wound Healing. a) Representative photographs of MRSA-infected wounds after treatments on days 0–10. b) Traces of wound-bed closure on days 0–10. c) Statistical analysis of relative bacterial viability of MRSA according to the plate colony counting on day 10. d) Wound areas statistics in treated differently. e) Statistical analysis of length of tissue gap. f) Images of agar plates with MRSA on days 1 and 10. g) HE staining images of wounds tissue treated differently on day 10 (green arrows: tissue gap). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Histological staining to evaluate wound healing. a) Characteristics of collagen deposition and pro-inflammatory factors. Images of Masson’s trichrome staining and the IHC for CD31, TNF-α and IL-6 on granulation tissues of different groups. Scale bar: 100 μm. b) Statistical analysis of collagen deposition. c) Statistical analysis of the number of CD31+ blood vessels. Statistical analysis of d) TNF-α and d) IL-6 of different groups. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 and ns: no significant.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the CQDs/hydrogel and its mechanisms in MRSA-infected wound healing.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon quantum dots decorated self-healing hydrogel for highly effective treatment of superbug infected wounds

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Rehabilitation, The Third Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Rehabilitation, The Third Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nursing Department, the Second Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nursing Department, the Second Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410011, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: SL.L. and ZZ.C. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid healing of wounds infected by bacteria, especially multidrug-resistant bacteria, is a common clinical challenge. Local bacterial infection causes excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) that further induce oxidative stress and inflammatory responses, which impairs cell migration, angiogenesis and delays cutaneous regeneration. Herein, we designed a novel type of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from herbal medicines. The CQDs are able to against gram-positive bacteria including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and possessed SOD-like as well as CAT-like activities for scavenging ROS. The biocompatible CQDs could protect HUVECs migration and angiogenesis from oxidative stress damage. Moreover, the expression of NO and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) of LPS-induced macrophages could be downregulated by the CQDs. Besides, the self-healing hydrogel decorated with multifunctional CQDs were prepared via the reaction between OSA and CMCS through Schiff base linkage for better wound management. The CQDs/hydrogel could continuously eliminate bacteria and scavenge ROS to modulate the complex oxidative stress microenvironment. The results of animal experiments demonstrated that the CQDs/hydrogel exerted the effects of eliminating MRSA, anti-inflammation, and promoting angiogenesis, which was manifested in enhancing wound healing, epithelial regeneration and collagen disposition. Therefore, the CQDs/hydrogel is excellent candidate for MRSA-infected skin wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As the first physiological barrier of the human body, skin plays significant roles such as protection, excretion, regulating body temperature, and sensing external stimuli. It is in direct contact with the outside environment and is more susceptible to serious damage[1–3]. The trauma will result in the loss of barrier defensive function of skin. Therefore, cutaneous wounds are commonly susceptible to bacterial infection due to the loss of inherent protective function of skin, which could further delay the process of wound healing[4–6]. Especially, the conventional antibiotics gradually become inefficient and face the problem of being phased out because of the antibiotic-resistant pathogenic bacteria including the commonly MRSA[7,8]. In addition, the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which further induces oxidative stress and excessive inflammation occurs frequently around the wound environment for protecting wounds from infection[9]. However, excessive oxidative stress delays the process of cutaneous regeneration due to the cell oxidative injury[10]. As a result, it is urgent for the development of novel and multifunctional treatment modalities for the management of infectious wounds.

Research in recent years has shown that carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have become a promising biomedical material, mainly due to their manufacturing process, structure, and physical and chemical properties[11–14]. CQDs are ultra-small 0-dimensional nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm. They have excellent optical properties, catalytic properties, especially good biocompatibility, and have great potential in antibacterial treatment[15–17]. Additionally, the nanozyme activities of CQDs have gradually attracted attention for scavenging ROS and free radicals[18,19]. Recently, we have also observed the development of nanozymes-based carbon quantum dots with antioxidant enzyme activity and their application in the treatment of inflammatory diseases[20–22]. Therefore, the synthesis of CQDs with the antibacterial and antioxidative abilities is promising strategy for wound healing. With the development of this field, the herbal medicines-derived CQDs have been applied for disease treatment due to the inspiration of traditional Chinese medicine[23–25]. The traditional Chinese medicine decoction of Wuwei Xiaodu Drink consisting of honeysuckle, wild chrysanthemum, dandelion, viola phillipina, and semiaquilegia adoxoide is an effective traditional Chinese medicine for treating acute surgical infections and infectious diseases[26]. However, the production of oral decoction is complex and there is a first-pass effect during the passage of decoctions through the gastrointestinal tract[27]. Inspired by this, we further synthesized CQDs derived from these above five kinds of herbs and investigated the effects on promoting infected wound healing by means of external administration.

For better wounds management, hydrogel dressings are applied to maintain a moist wound environment, absorb tissue exudates, make oxygen permeate, cool wounds and alleviate the pain of patients[28]. In particular, injectable hydrogel dressings with self-healing ability possess exclusive characteristics, including drugs that are coated in-situ, the ability to fill irregular wounds, the capacity to adhere wounds together, and high durability[29]. Sodium alginate (SA) and Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS), natural biopolymers with antibacterial ability and abundant sources, have been widely adopted for biomedical dressings[30]. The SA is oxidized into active aldehyde groups and then cross-linked with the free amine groups of CMCS through dynamic imine bonds to form a self-healing injectable hydrogel. They are further mixed with nanoparticles to achieve multifunctional applications[31,32]. However, few studies have been reported that the natural polymers decorated with multifunctional CQDs as a strategy to improve wound healing.

Five herbal medicines were purchased from the department of Chinese pharmacy of the third Xiangya hospital. Chitosan (degree of deacetylation 85%, Mw = 70 kDa), sodium alginate (viscosity 200±20 mPa s, Mw = 32 kDa), sodium periodate (NaIO4), and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Cell counting kit-8 was purchased from Targetmol. USA. SOD Activity Assay Kit and Catalase Assay Kit were purchased from Beijing Boxbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Calcein AM/PI double stain kit, DCFH-DA probe, and DAF-FM DA probe were purchased from Beijing Beyotime Biotechnology Company. Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923), MRSA (ATCC 43300) and Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922) were provided by the department of Clinical Laboratory of the third Xiangya hospital. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and RAW264.7 cells were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Sciences and stored in F12/DMEM supplemented with 1% penicillin − streptomycin (Fisher, USA) and 10% fetal bovine serum (Fisher, USA) in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C.

The herbal powders (15 g in total) were mixed according to the certain ratio of honeysuckle, wild chrysanthemum, dandelion, viola phillipina, and semiaquilegia adoxoide (honeysuckle: chrysanthemum: dandelion: viola phillipina: semiaquilegia adoxoide = 2.5:1:1:1:1). After adding 150 mL ultrapure water, the reaction mixture was stirred to fully mix and then sonicated for 20 min. The resulting mixture was transferred into a stainless-steel reaction vessel with polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered by a circulating water vacuum pump with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the filtrate was transferred to a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 48 h. The water was replaced every 6 h. Following completion of dialysis, the CQDs powder was obtained following freeze-drying. The other five kinds of CQDs were synthesized from five individual herbs according to the above experiments.

A certain amount of CQDs (2 mg/ml) was mixed with 1 mL of 8% (w/v) OSA in PBS, then 1 mL of 5% (w/v) CMCS was added. Using a vortex mixer to distribute the mixture evenly and pour into molds at room temperature. After 10 min of gelation, check the performance of the hydrogel. Hydrogels can be molded into different shapes and used in different experiments.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F) was used to observe the microscopic morphology of materials. The UV–vis spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) of the carbon dots was tested with an F-700 spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by Thermo Scientific EscaLab 250Xi. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Rigaku Ultimate Iv diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The structure and morphology of hydrogel were obtained with a scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Rheological analysis of hydrogel was performed on an RS6000 rotational rheometer (HAAKE, Karlsruhe, Germany) using parallel plates at room temperature.

The 1.5×107 CFU/mL bacterial suspension was smeared evenly on the MH agar plates (diameter = 90 mm) by using sterile medical swabs, and the disks containing a certain amount of CQDs or PBS were placed onto the above plates. After 20 h of incubation at 37 °C in a biochemicalincubator, the diameter of the inhibition zone around each disk was measured and recorded.

The 200 μL of PBS, hydrogel, CQDs solution and CQDs/hydrogel was added to the 48-well plate. After gelation for 30 min, a 20 μL bacterial suspension (in LB, 1.5×108 CFU/mL) was added to the wells containing the solutions or hydrogels. After the plates are placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 24 h, 1 mL of sterilized PBS was added to each well to re-suspend any bacteria alive. Bacterial colonies on the LB plates were counted after incubation at 37 °C for 20 h.
The bacteria cocultured with above different treatments were stained with SYTO 9 and PI for 15 min in the dark. Then the mixture was observed by inverted fluorescent microscopy.

ABTS+ free radical working solution was form by a 7.4 mM ABTS diammonium salt solution and a 2.6 mM potassium persulfate solution were mixed together in aqueous solution and protected from light for 12 h. After diluting the ABTS+ free radical working solution with 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.4), CQDs solutions of different concentrations were added, and the reaction was conducted in the dark. The ABTS+ radical scavenging rate was determined based on the absorbance measured at a wavelength of 734 nm.

The SOD-enzyme-like activity of CQDs was measured using a SOD activity assay kit. According to the instructions in the kit, CQDs solutions of different concentrations react with the •O2 − radical test solution at 25 °C for 30 min. The absorbance at 560 nm was measured to determine the •O2 − radical scavenging efficiency.

The H2O2 scavenging activity of CQDs was measured using a CAT assay kit. According to the instructions in the kit, CQD solutions of different concentrations react with the H2O2 test solution at 25 °C for 10 min. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured to determine the H2O2 scavenging efficiency.

HUVEC and RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 8×103 cells per well. After overnight culturing, the cells were added with different concentrations of CQDs for 24 h incubation. Then, the medium was replaced by 100 μL of CCK-8 solution followed by a 2 h incubation, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured to quantify the cell viability. For the Live/Dead assay, the HUVECs received the above treatment and were then stained according to the manufacturer’s protocol. The stained cells were further imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope. The HUVECs received the above treatment were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min and washed three times with PBS. They were incubated with Ki67 antibody at 4 °C overnight and then incubated with CoraLite488-conjugated Goat anti-rabbit IgG antibody at 1:200 dilution for 1 h. Before imaging with inverted fluorescent microscopy, the cells were incubated with DAPI for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory activity test
ce:section.ce:para: The universal fluorescent probe, 2′,7′--dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), was used to detect the total amount of intracellular ROS. DCFH can be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterase to generate DCFH. Since DCFH could not penetrate cell membranes, the probe can be easily loaded into cells. Intracellular ROS can oxidize DCFH to generate fluorescent DCF. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of DCF reflects the level of intracellular ROS. HUVEC or RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 8×103 cells per well. After overnight culture, the cells were added with different concentrations of CQDs for 24 h incubation. Then, H2O2 (400 μM) was added for 1 h at 37 °C to introduce the intracellular oxidative stress of HUVECs. Next, the HUVECs in the dark and incubated with DCFH-DA probe for 30 min. Before fluorescence images were obtained by inverted fluorescent microscopy, the cells were washed three times with PBS. Meanwhile, LPS (5 μg/mL) was added for 24 h to increase the intracellular oxidative stress of RAW264.7 cells. Subsequently, the cells in the dark were incubated with DAF-FM-DA probe for 20 min and then washed three times with PBS before fluorescence images were obtained by inverted fluorescent microscopy. Moreover, the supernatant RAW264.7 cells after different treatments was collected, and the concentrations of proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) in the supernatant were quantified using the corresponding ELISA quantification kits according to the manufacturer’s protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Migration and angiogenesis tests of HUVECs
ce:section.ce:para: Cell scratch assays were used to evaluate the effect of CQDs on HUVECs migration. After HUVECs were transplanted into six-well plates until they completely covered each well, different concentrations of CQDs were added and incubated for 24 h. Next, H2O2 (400 μM) was added for 1 h and the monolayer was then scraped with a 10 μL pipette tip. After 48 h, optical images were taken by inverted fluorescent microscopy. The possibility of tube formation of HUVECs after different treatments was examined by using Matrigel. The cells were trypsinized and inoculated into Matrigel at 3×104 cells/well in 48-well plates for 4 h. Subsequently, optical images were taken by inverted fluorescent microscopy and then analyzed by an Image J angiogenesis analyzer.

All animal experiment protocols in this study were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Central South University. Male SD rats (10 weeks old) were purchased from the Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. (Changsha China). Recipient rats were anesthetized through nebulized isofluorane and oxygen gas mixture administered via inhalatory route. After shaving the upper back region and sterilized with 75% ethanol of the rats, then the round whole skin with a diameter of about 1.5 cm was excised. 100 µL of 1×108 CFU mL−1 fresh MRSA suspension was injected into the surgical area after surgery. After 24 h of continuous infection, animals randomly divided into four groups: control group, hydrogel, CQDs and CQDs/hydrogel treatment groups. Wound healing process of these different groups was monitored with a light microscope for up to10 days and were photographed on days 0, 2, 4, 8, and 10. The wound healing rate at each time point was calculated as follows: wound healing rate (%) = [(original wound area—remaining wound area obtained at each time period)/original wound area] × 100%. Moreover, bacteria in infected areas were also collected with sterile medical swabs and homogenized on days 1 and 10. These samples were diluted in the same gradient with PBS, then evenly distributed on agar plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C. The in vivo antibacterial activity of CQDs/hydrogel was reflected by bacterial colony counts. After 10 days, the rats were sacrificed and wound tissues were harvested. These wound tissues were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde overnight for further histological and immunofluorescence analysis.

Statistical analysis of all data is presented as the mean ± standard deviation. Significant differences among groups were determined by Student’s two-tailed t-test. Values of P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized based on five herbal powders through a solvothermal method at 200 °C for 6 h. The morphology and size of the prepared CQDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the CQDs have uniform size and good dispersibility, with an average particle size of 2.62±0.65 nm (Fig. 1c). According to the TEM (Fig. 1b), the CQDs also have good crystallinity with obvious lattice fringes, and the interplanar spacing is measured as about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (001) crystal plane of carbon. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited one tiny absorption band at 285 nm (Fig. 1d), which is attributed to the p–p* transition of the sp2 carbon domains. The CQDs solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence when excited at 365 nm (Fig. 1d). Meanwhile, it displayed excitation-dependent characteristics (Fig. S2a). When CQDs were excited at 360 nm, they emitted strong PL in the range of 375–700 nm, with the peak intensity occurring around 460 nm.

The chemical composition and functional groups of CQDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 1e displayed peaks at 3378, 2932,1662, 1607, and 1074 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH, C-H, C=O, C=C, and C-O groups, respectively. Therefore, the remarkable water solubility of the CQDs could be attributed to the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups on their surfaces. Subsequently, the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 1f) indicated the CQDs, mainly consist of C, N, and O element. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be resolved into three peaks with binding energies of approximately284.5, 285.9, and 287.8 eV. These peaks correspond to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1g). The N1s band was deconvoluted into peaks at 397.8, 399.6, and 401.2 eV, which correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1h). The peaks of the O1s spectrum at 531.3, and 532.2 eV belonged to C-OH/C-O-C, and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1i). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern showed clear peaks, indicating that the CQDs were pure and successfully prepared. Specifically, the peak observed at approximately 21° corresponds to the (001) crystal plane of carbon (Fig. S2b).

The synthesis process of hydrogel dressing was displayed in Scheme 1. First of all, to introduce an aldehyde group to sodium alginate (SA), sodium periodate was employed for the oxidation of sodium alginate, resulting in the formation of oxidized sodium alginate (OSA). Then, OSA was mixed with CQDs, which serves as a multifunctional active substance. The mixture was reacted with CMCS through the Schiff base linkage between primary amine and aldehyde (Fig. 2 a). In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3), the successful preparation of OSA was shown by the aldehyde peak at 1733 cm−1. Schiff base reaction occurred between the aldehyde group of OSA and the amino group of CMCS according to the characteristic absorption of imine stretching vibration (-C=N-) at 1589 cm−1. The schematic diagram and photos of the self-healing process between the OSA/CMCS hydrogel and CQDs/OSA/CMCS hydrogel were shown in Fig. 2b. Two cylindrical hydrogels with or without CQDs were cut in half and fused together by contact. In this study, three hydrogel samples contained different concentration of OSA (8%, 6% and 4%) were investigated for the gelation time (Fig. S4, Fig. 2e). The gelation time of three kinds of hydrogel samples were around 2.5 min, 12.2 min and 17.3 min according to the storage module (G') was equal to the loss modulus (G''), respectively[33]. The adjustable gelation time could be used in a variety of applications according to clinical requirements. For further analysis, OSA/CMCS hydrogels with an OSA concentration of 8% were prepared. As shown in Fig. 2f, the hydrogel was smoothly and continuously injected into the culture dish using a syringe without needle clogging during the extrusion process.

To understand the injectability of hydrogel, the dependence of viscosity on shear rate was studied. The viscosity of the hydrogel significantly decreased with increasing shear rate, which indicated it could be injected into any irregular area and gelled in situ to fill the lesion site. Rheological tests were performed to further analyze the self-healing properties of OSA/CMCS hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 2g, the G' of the OSA/CMCS hydrogel at 1% strain was greater than the G'', indicating that the hydrogel was in a gel state. When the strain was immediately increased to 700%, the G′ of the hydrogel drops from 259 Pa to 92 Pa immediately. This means that the network structure of the hydrogel was destroyed at this time. However, when the strain was restored to 1%, the G′ value returned to its original value. The repeated fracture and recovery behaviors demonstrated the rapid and effective self-healing ability of the hydrogel.

Due to the appropriate degradation time can affect the process of tissue repair and the release of contents, the degradability of hydrogel is crucial for tissue repair and regeneration[34]. Fig. S5 was the degradation behavior curves of OSA/CMCS hydrogel in PBS at pH 7.4. The results showed that the remaining weight ratio of OSA/CMCS hydrogel was reduced to about 36% within 48 h, which was useful for wound management and drug release. The prepared CQDs/hydrogel possessed uniformly distributed large and small pores according to the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the cross-sections of the CQDs/hydrogel were shown in Fig. 2c. The pore size of hydrogel and CQDs/hydrogel was 342.29±79.58 μm and 100.37±28.04 μm, respectively. It indicated that the crosslinking density is higher after adding CQDs. Considering the application of tissue engineering to promote cell proliferation, oxygen and nutrient transport, this interconnected porous structure is an important factor[35]. As shown in Fig. 2d, there were obvious fluorescent signal in the CQDs/hydrogel, which indicated the CQDs were evenly distributed in the hydrogel system owning to their excellent water solubility. Therefore, the hydrogel can enhance wound healing as a result of the porous structures are favorable to the wound exudates absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-bacterial and anti-oxidative capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The disk-diffusion test was enforced to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the CQDs. These disks contained PBS at pH 7.4 or six kinds of CQDs were placed on MHA plates containing three kinds of bacteria (S. aureus (ATCC25923), MRSA (ATCC43300) and E. coli (ATCC25922)). Interestingly, we found that the CQDs derived from the proportional mixing of five kinds of herbs could against gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and MRSA) compared to other five kinds of CQDs derived from individual herbs (Fig. S8). Then, obvious inhibition zones were observed on agar plates incubated with S. aureus and MRSA (Figs. S8 and S9). The mean inhibitory zone diameter of the MRSA plate was 13.7 mm, which was larger than that of the S. aureus plate (approximately 12.2 mm). However, almost zone of inhibition was not observed on the agar plates cultured with E. coli, which indicated that the antibacterial effect of all of these six kinds of CQDs on Gram-positive Staphylococcus was specific and effective, especially on MRSA. The structure of E. coli is more complex than that of S. aureus may could explain the antibacterial differences[36]. Moreover, the antibacterial activity of the CQDs/hydrogel was detected using the colony-forming unit (CFU) method. The obtained colony growth photographs were shown in Fig. 3 a1 and b1. Compared with the PBS, hydrogel and CQDs groups, bacteria in the CQDs/hydrogel groups were obviously unable to survive. Under experimental conditions, the survival rate of the CQDs/hydrogel group against S. aureus and MRSA was less than 1% (Fig. 3c and d). The antibacterial ability of CQDs/hydrogel was further evaluated by the live/dead cell experiments through using SYTO 9/PI staining. Bacteria in the control group showed clear green fluorescence. After adding hydrogel and CQDs, the red fluorescence gradually enhanced. Among them, the red fluorescence of the CQDs/hydrogel treatment group was strongest, and the green fluorescence was almost invisible, indicating the elimination effect on both bacteria (Fig. 3a2 and 3b2).

During the wound healing process, overexpression of free radicals can cause uncontrolled inflammatory responses and severely hinder tissue repair. Therefore, hydrogels decorated with antioxidant have great potential in promoting wound healing[37]. The total free radical scavenging ability of CQDs was systematically studied through ABTS radials scavenging tests. Since ABTS radials are inhibited in the presence of antioxidants, the antioxidant capacity can be determined by calculating the absorbance of ABTS solution at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. S10, the degree of ABTS radicals inhibition increased with increasing CQDs concentration. When the concentration of CQDs is 12 μg/mL (Fig. 3e), the inhibition rate reaches more than 90%, indicating that CQDs had the excellent ability to scavenge free radicals. The SOD-like activity of CQDs was then examined by using an SOD kit. •O2 − was generated via the reaction of xanthine/xanthine oxidase system. As a result, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) could be reduced to formazan with a strong absorbance at 560 nm[38]. The results revealed that CQDs had good •O2 − scavenging ability and dose-dependent scavenging ability (Fig. S11). The inhibition ratio of •O2 − was around 76% when the CQDs concentration reached 80 μg/mL (Fig. 3f). The CAT-like activity of CQDs was also evaluated by monitoring the decomposition of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3g, approximately 82% of the total H2O2 was decomposed by 200 μg/mL of CQDs. Therefore, the CQD materials can serve as both SOD mimic and CAT mimic. They can convert •O2 − into H2O2 and O2, while promote the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2. Interestingly, the CQDs with good ROS scavenging ability exhibited lower cytotoxicity than more common metal-based nano-enzymes, such as Prussian blue nanozymes, MOF, metal carbon quantum dots and other metal-based materials[20,39–42]. As a result, the excessive ROS can be removed by CQDs in the environment of wounds in time for enhancing tissue regeneration[43]. These results confirmed that CQDs exhibited good anti-bacterial and anti-oxidative abilities for promoting wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, intracellular anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CQDs was tested through the standard CCK-8 assay. Fig. 4 a and b indicated that CQDs had no obvious cytotoxicity to human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and RAW 264.7 within 800 μg/mL after 24 h of incubation, respectively. Almost no dead cells were observed according to the live/dead cell staining results of the HUVECs cultured with CQDs for 24 h were shown in Fig. 4d. Afterward, Ki67, an antigen associated with the phases of the cell cycle, was used to explore the detailed impact of CQDs on the proliferation of HUVECs[44]. The results showed that the proliferation of HUVECs with bright green fluorescence treated with various concentrations of CQDs was almost the same as that of control group. Additionally, the quantitative analysis data revealed that the ratio of Ki67+ cells in all treatment groups exceeded 70%. The above results demonstrated that the CQDs have good biocompatibility for further clinical practice.

Then, the intracellular antioxidative activity of the CQDs was studied. The results of Fig. 5 a demonstrated that the intracellular ROS level of H2O2-stimulated HUVECs treated with CQDs showed lower fluorescence than positive control group. This result indicated that CQDs can effectively catalyze the decomposition of intracellular H2O2 in a concentration-dependent manner. As shown in Fig. 5b, CQDs could decrease the level of NO of RAW264.7 stimulated by lipopolysaccharide (LPS). After tissue damage, pro-inflammatory mediators (TNF-α and IL-6) that were important for the process of wound healing were assessed by the enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA)[45]. LPS stimulates RAW264.7 to release various inflammatory cytokines. The levels of TNF-α (Fig. 5e) and IL-6 (Fig. 5f) were significantly reduced in the CQDs pre-treatment group compared with the untreated group. In addition, the CQDs reduced inflammatory cytokines in a dose-dependent manner. As a result, the CQDs has an excellent antioxidative function, anti-inflammatory effect, and outstanding potential in the treatment of wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell migration and angiogenesis
ce:section.ce:para: Newly formed vessels can deliver nutrition and oxygen to wounds so as to accelerate wound closure, which can be delayed by excessive ROS in wound microenvironment[43]. To determine whether the ROS scavenging ability of the CQDs could protect the migration and angiogenesis ability of HUVECs from oxidative stress, the scratch and tube formation assays were performed (Fig. 5g and h). After 48 h of incubation, the migration ratios of HUVECs were significantly higher in these groups with different concentrations of CQDs than in the positive control groups, and the results of the group with highest concentration of CQDs (100 μg/mL) were close to that of the negative control group (Fig. 5i). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5h, the CQDs groups presented higher efficiency than the H2O2 groups in promoting tube formation in a concentration-dependent manner, characterized by more visible tube-like structures, higher junction numbers, and longer capillary length (Fig. 5j and k). The results above indicated that CQDs can facilitate the formation of new blood vessels and have the potential for promoting wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo CQDs/hydrogel promotes MRSA-infected wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs/hydrogel, CQDs, hydrogel, and PBS as the control group were performed to investigate their curative effect in wound healing in vivo. MRSA was first injected into the full-thickness rat skin defect with a diameter of 1.5 cm. After 24 h, the wounds suffered from serious infections followed by different treatments. The wound sizes in all the tested rats gradually decreased over time according to the process of wound healing that was monitored daily and imaged on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 (Fig. 6 a). On day 10, the wound healing rate treated with CQDs/hydrogel were almost completely healed (about 98.2%) and new skin tissue was shown. The wound healing ratios of rats in the control, hydrogel, and CQDs treatment groups were around 75.1%,81.3%, and 90.1%, respectively (Fig. 6d). And traces of wound areas during 10 days for each treatment are also drawn (Fig. 6b). Additionally, local bacteria from all groups of wounds on days 1 and 10 were recorded to assess the in vivo antibacterial activity of the CQDs/hydrogel (Fig. 6c and f). The number of MRSA in wounds treated by CQDs/hydrogel was significantly less than that of other groups, which demonstrated that CQDs/hydrogel have the potential for managing wound infection and accelerating wound healing.

For further exploring the complex biological processes including inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling during the period of infection wound healing treated with CQDs/hydrogel, we evaluated the histological changes after 10 days. The results of H&E shown in Fig. 6g indicated that several inflammatory cells were observed in the tissues of the control group compared with that of CQDs/hydrogel group that had weaker inflammatory cell infiltration and more fibroblast migration. And the length of tissue gap of different groups was consistent with their wound areas shown in Fig. 6a (Fig. 6e). The length of tissue gap of CQDs/hydrogel was shortest which represented the effect of epithelial regeneration of this group was best. On the 10th day, only some loose and messy collagen fibers appeared in the control group. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the collagen deposition in the CQD/hydrogel group was higher, and the collagen fibers in the skin tissue were denser, thicker, and orderly. CD31, a typical marker of vascular endothelial cells, plays a crucial role in angiogenesis[46]. The content of CD31 was higher in CQDs and CQDs/hydrogel groups than the other groups on day 10 (Fig. 7a). Whenthe number of CD31+ blood vessels in the samearea oftissue sectionswas counted,the number of blood vessels (marked by red arrows) inthe CQDs/hydrogel groupwas higherthanthat inother groups (Fig. 7c). After the generation of acute wounds and stimulation of the infectious environment, cells secrete excessive proinflammatory factors including TNF-α and IL-6 that delay wound healing. The immunohistochemical staining results for the inflammatory marker TNF-α and IL-6 indicated that the expression of them in the wounds treated with CQDs/hydrogel were significantly lower than those in other groups (Fig. 7a, d and e). As a result, the multifunctional CQDs/hydrogel can serve as an effective dressing to enhance wound healing by combating bacteria, promoting angiogenesis and regulating inflammatory factors in vivo.

In summary, the distinctive CQDs were synthesized from herbal medicines and their potential of promoting wound healing was assessed in this work. The as-prepared CQDs not only directly against gram-positive bacterial including MRSA and unlike other reported nanomaterials which need the help of an external light source, but had satisfactory SOD-like and CAT-like enzymatic activities for scavenging a wide variety of free radicals. In vitro cell function experiments indicated that the biocompatible CQDs could scavenge intracellular ROS sufficiently for protecting biological functions of cells from excessive oxidative stress damage. Moreover, the CQDs exhibited anti-inflammatory ability through decreasing the levels of NO and inflammatory factors of LPS-induced macrophages. Besides, we fabricated a self-healing hydrogel consisting of OSA-CHO and CMCS-amino and based on Schiff base bonds to load the multifunctional CQDs. The CQDs/hydrogel could adapt to various complex and irregular wounds as well as persistently release the therapeutic CQDs over time. In the MRSA-infected skin defect model, the CQDs/hydrogel leaded to a faster healing process in areas of wound through reducing the degree of infection, regulating inflammation, promoting the formation of new blood vessels and son on. However, for better clinical application, the yield of the CQDs should be further improved, and the anti-bacterial mechanisms of the CQDs should also further explored. Overall, the convenient, safe, low-cost and effective CQDs/hydrogel system shows great potential in clinical applications for the management of infectious wounds.
Data availability statement.
All the other data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary material files and from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Shengli Lu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Zhizhao Chen: Investigation, Methodology. Hanyu Tu: Methodology. Huaxin Liu: Methodology, Resources. Yang Liu: Methodology. Shuyue Chen: Methodology. Dongyang Cai: Methodology. Can Liu: Methodology. Xiangyan Zhang: Funding acquisition. Guoqiang Zou: Resources. Huiqing Xie: Resources. Xiaobo Ji: Project administration, Resources. Hongshuai Hou: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianda Zhou: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81872219, 52074359), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2020JJ4828), Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014), Scientific Key Research Project of Health Commission of Hunan Province (C202304109575).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.148218.

ce:section.ce:para:
